plainMeaning: A Jain householder here admits the fifteen kinds of harmful livelihood — trades and occupations that kill many living beings — and asks forgiveness for any he may have practised or supported.
recitation: ॥ Karmato talāra, guptapāla pramukha khara karma ane ājīvikā arthe kuvyavasāya parihaṛuṁ ॥ Karmato e vratataṇā pandara atichāra śodhuṁ. Iṅgālakamme, vaṇakamme, sāḍīkamme, bhāḍīkamme, phoḍīkamme, dantavāṇijje, lakkhavāṇijje, rasavāṇijje, visavāṇijje, kesavāṇijje, jantapilaṇakamme, nilaṁchaṇakamme, davaggi dāvaṇayā, sara-daha talāva sosaṇayā, asai posaṇayā. 1. Iṅgālakamme — lābhārthe rāṅgaṇa, līhālā, sonārā, kaṁsārā, ṭhaṁṭhārā, bhāḍabhuṁjā, iṁṭavāha, nīmāha, dhātu-dhamanādika agnikarma kīdhāṁ hoy. 2. Vaṇakamme — kaṇa, kapāśiyā, phūla, phala, pānataṇo vikraya kīdho hoy, dalāvaṇuṁ, loḍhāvaṇuṁ maṁḍāvyuṁ hoy; vāṁsa, valī, kāṭha vaḍhāvyā phaḍāvyā hoy. 3. Śakaṭakamme — gāḍāvāhinī dharī, ūdhī, paitaṇo vikraya kīdho hoy. 4. Bhāṭakamme — poṭhīyā, vahītrā, ūṁṭa, baḷada, khara, vesara, śakaṭataṇuṁ bhāruṁ kīdhuṁ hoy. 5. Phoṭikamme — ājivikārthe khāṇa, pāṣāṇa, māṭī, muraḍa khaṇāvyā hoy, karasaṇa kīdhuṁ hoy. 6. Dantavāṇijje — āgara jaī gajadanta, camara, kasturī, nakha, roma, carma līdhāṁ hoy. 7. Lakkhavāṇijje — lākha, gaḷī, maṇasila, dhāuḍī, mahuḍā, sājī, tūrī, sākaroḍa, bhāṁga, sābū, kandādika vahoryāṁ hoy. 8. Rasavāṇijje — rasa, madha, madhāṁga, madhu, mākhaṇa, veśaṇātaṇo vikraya kīdho hoy. 9. Visavāṇijje — viṣa, haḷa, hathiyāra, lohayantra, hariyāla, kāṁkasī pramukha jīvaghātaka vastu veṁcī veṁcāvī hoy. 10. Kesavāṇijje — dvipada, catuṣpadataṇo vikraya kīdho hoy. 11. Yantrapīlaṇakamme — arahaṭṭa, pāvaṭṭa, kolahuṁ, loḍhaṇa, ūkhala, mūśala, ghaṁṭī, ghāṇī vāhyā hoy. 12. Nillaṁchaṇakamme — dvipada, catuṣpada pratye āṁka, ḍāṁbha, pāchaṇāṁ, karṇa, kaṁbala, muṣkacchheda, pṛṣṭhi-gālana, nāsā-vedhādika kīdhāṁ karāvyāṁ hoy. 13. Davaggi-dāvaṇayā — khaḍa annataṇī ghaṇī niṣpattibhaṇī dava dīdhāṁ, devarāvyāṁ hoy. 14. Sara-daha talāva sosaṇayā — sara śoṣāvyāṁ hoy, bhilla kheḍāvyā hoy, kyārā gaharāvyā hoy, draha, talāva phoḍāvyāṁ hoy. 15. Asai poṣaṇayā — śvāna, biḍāla, śūḍā sālaḍhī, mora, kurkuṭa, durācāra, dāsa, dāsaru poṣyāṁ hoy, anerāṁ bahu pāpa vyāpāra vyavasāya kīdhāṁ hoy. E pannare karmādāna viṣe sthūla-niyama sūkṣmataṇī jayaṇā. E pannare atiyāramāṁhe ॥

wordByWord.gloss: from the standpoint of livelihood/action (karmataḥ) — i.e. the 'work' half of the 7th vow, as against bhojanataḥ ('food' half) of the preceding verse
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit karman ('act, occupation') + ablative -taḥ ('with respect to').
wordByWord.notes: Pairs with the previous verse's bhojanataḥ: the Bhogopabhoga vow is reviewed first by food, now by livelihood.

wordByWord.gloss: town-watchman / police-officer (a harsh, life-harming office)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit talāra ('city-guard, kotwal'); cf. Sanskrit talāraka.

wordByWord.gloss: jailer / keeper of a prison
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit gupta ('confined, secret') + pāla ('keeper').

wordByWord.gloss: and the like, et cetera (lit. 'foremost, chief')
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pra- + mukha ('face, head'); idiomatic 'and such others'.

wordByWord.gloss: harsh / cruel occupations
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit khara ('sharp, harsh') + karman ('work').

wordByWord.gloss: and
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati conjunction, from Sanskrit anya / Apabhraṁśa aṇe.

wordByWord.gloss: for the sake of livelihood
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ājīvikā ('means of living') + arthe ('for the purpose of').

wordByWord.gloss: wrong / evil business pursuits
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ku- ('bad') + vyavasāya ('enterprise, occupation').

wordByWord.gloss: I renounce / I shun
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit pari-√hṛ ('to set aside, abandon'); 1st-person Gujarati present.

wordByWord.gloss: of this vow (the 7th, Bhogopabhoga)
wordByWord.etymology: e ('this') + vrata ('vow') + Old-Gujarati genitive -taṇā ('belonging to').

wordByWord.gloss: fifteen
wordByWord.etymology: Gujarati pandara, from Sanskrit pañcadaśa.

wordByWord.gloss: transgressions (here the fifteen karmādāna)
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit ati- ('over') + cāra ('going'). Spelled અતિચાર here, though the book's norm is અતિયાર.
wordByWord.notes: This verse exceptionally writes અતિચાર (with ca); §7 of the tradition base notes the book's usual spelling is અતિયાર — both preserved verbatim.

wordByWord.gloss: I purify / I scrutinize and cleanse
wordByWord.etymology: Sanskrit √śudh / śodhana ('to purify'); the standard atiyāra-formula verb.

wordByWord.gloss: the work of (making) charcoal / fire-based trades (1st karmādāna)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit iṅgāla ('charcoal, ember', Skt. aṅgāra) + kamme ('in the work', Skt. karman, locative).

wordByWord.gloss: forest/vegetation work — felling and trading plant life (2nd)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit vaṇa ('forest', Skt. vana) + kamme ('work').

wordByWord.gloss: cart-work — making/selling carts (3rd; = śakaṭa-karma, so named below)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sāḍī/sakaḍa ('cart', Skt. śakaṭa) + kamme.
wordByWord.notes: The book's numbered gloss renames it શકટકમ્મે (śakaṭakamme).

wordByWord.gloss: hire/porterage work — letting out beasts and carts for loads (4th; = bhāṭaka-karma)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit bhāḍa/bhāḍī ('hire, fare', Skt. bhāṭaka) + kamme.

wordByWord.gloss: breaking/digging work — mining, quarrying, ploughing the earth (5th; = sphoṭaka-karma)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit phoḍa ('splitting, bursting', Skt. sphoṭa) + kamme.

wordByWord.gloss: ivory trade (6th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit danta ('tusk, tooth') + vāṇijja ('trade', Skt. vāṇijya), locative.

wordByWord.gloss: lac trade (7th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit lakkha ('lac-resin', Skt. lākṣā) + vāṇijja.

wordByWord.gloss: trade in liquids — liquor, fat, honey, butter (8th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit rasa ('juice, liquid') + vāṇijja.

wordByWord.gloss: trade in poisons and weapons (9th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit visa ('poison', Skt. viṣa) + vāṇijja.

wordByWord.gloss: trade in living beings — bipeds and quadrupeds (lit. 'hair-trade') (10th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit kesa ('hair', Skt. keśa) + vāṇijja; here glossed as dealing in two- and four-footed creatures.

wordByWord.gloss: machine-pressing work — mills, presses, oil-mills (11th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit janta ('machine', Skt. yantra) + pilaṇa ('pressing', Skt. pīḍana) + kamme.

wordByWord.gloss: mutilation work — castrating, branding, piercing animals (12th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit nillaṁchaṇa ('castration, marking', Skt. nirlāñchana) + kamme.

wordByWord.gloss: the setting of forest-fires (13th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit davaggi ('forest-fire', Skt. davāgni) + dāvaṇayā ('the act of giving/setting', Skt. dāpana).

wordByWord.gloss: the drying-up of lakes, pools and ponds (14th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit sara ('lake', Skt. saras) + daha ('deep pool', Skt. hrada) + talāva ('tank') + sosaṇayā ('drying', Skt. śoṣaṇa).

wordByWord.gloss: the rearing of the unchaste/immoral — keeping creatures or persons for sinful gain (15th)
wordByWord.etymology: Prakrit asaī ('unchaste, immoral', Skt. asatī) + posaṇayā ('nourishing', Skt. poṣaṇa).

wordByWord.token: ૧. લાભાર્થે રાંગણ, લીહાલા, સોનારા, કંસારા, ઠંઠારા, ભાડભુંજા, ઇંટવાહ, નીમાહ, ધાતુધમનાદિક અગ્નિકર્મ કીધાં હોય
wordByWord.translit: 1. lābhārthe rāṅgaṇa, līhālā, sonārā, kaṁsārā, ṭhaṁṭhārā, bhāḍabhuṁjā, iṁṭavāha, nīmāha, dhātu-dhamanādika agnikarma kīdhāṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (charcoal-work, glossed:) 'for profit, may have done fire-work — smelting, blacksmithing, goldsmithing, brazier/coppersmith work, tinkering, grain-roasting, brick-firing, lime-kilning, metal-melting and the like'
wordByWord.etymology: lābhārthe ('for gain') + trade-names + agni-karma ('fire-work') + kīdhāṁ hoy ('may have done').
wordByWord.notes: rāṅgaṇa = founding/smelting; līhālā = lohāra (smith); sonārā = goldsmith; kaṁsārā = brazier; ṭhaṁṭhārā = tinker; bhāḍabhuṁjā = grain-parcher; iṁṭavāha = brick-baker; nīmāha = lime-kiln.

wordByWord.token: ૨. કણ, કપાશિયા, ફૂલ, ફલ, પાનતણો વિક્રય... વાંસ, વલી, કાઠ વઢાવ્યા ફડાવ્યા હોય
wordByWord.translit: 2. kaṇa, kapāśiyā, phūla, phala, pānataṇo vikraya... vāṁsa, valī, kāṭha vaḍhāvyā phaḍāvyā hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (forest-work, glossed:) 'may have sold grain, cotton, flowers, fruit and leaves; had grinding and threshing set up; had bamboo, creeper and timber cut and split'
wordByWord.etymology: vikraya ('sale, trade'); vaḍhāvyā phaḍāvyā ('had cut, had split').

wordByWord.token: ૩. ગાડાવાહિની ધરી, ઊધી, પઇતણો વિક્રય કીધો હોય
wordByWord.translit: 3. gāḍāvāhinī dharī, ūdhī, paitaṇo vikraya kīdho hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (cart-work, glossed:) 'may have traded in carts, axles, wheels and their parts'
wordByWord.etymology: gāḍāvāhinī ('cart-frame'); dharī/ūdhī/pai ('axle, hub, wheel-parts').

wordByWord.token: ૪. પોઠીયા, વહીત્રા, ઊંટ, બળદ, ખર, વેસર, શકટતણું ભારું કીધું હોય
wordByWord.translit: 4. poṭhīyā, vahītrā, ūṁṭa, baḷada, khara, vesara, śakaṭataṇuṁ bhāruṁ kīdhuṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (hire-work, glossed:) 'may have loaded burdens on pack-oxen, carriers, camels, bullocks, donkeys, mules and carts'
wordByWord.etymology: khara ('donkey'), vesara ('mule', Skt. veśara); bhāruṁ ('load').

wordByWord.token: ૫. આજિવિકાર્થે ખાણ, પાષાણ, માટી, મુરડ ખણાવ્યા હોય, કરસણ કીધું હોય
wordByWord.translit: 5. ājivikārthe khāṇa, pāṣāṇa, māṭī, muraḍa khaṇāvyā hoy, karasaṇa kīdhuṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (digging-work, glossed:) 'for livelihood, may have had mines, stone, earth and gravel dug, and done cultivation'
wordByWord.etymology: khaṇāvyā ('had dug', Skt. khanana); karasaṇa ('farming', Skt. karṣaṇa).

wordByWord.token: ૬. આગર જઇ ગજદંત, ચમર, કસ્તુરી, નખ, રોમ, ચર્મ લીધાં હોય
wordByWord.translit: 6. āgara jaī gajadanta, camara, kasturī, nakha, roma, carma līdhāṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (ivory-trade, glossed:) 'going to the source, may have taken elephant-tusks, yak-tail whisks, musk, claws, fur and hides'
wordByWord.etymology: āgara ('source, mine'); gaja-danta ('elephant-tusk'); carma ('hide').

wordByWord.token: ૭. લાખ, ગળી, મણસિલ, ધાઉડી, મહુડા, સાજી, તૂરી, સાકરોડ, ભાંગ, સાબૂ, કંદાદિક વહોર્યાં હોય
wordByWord.translit: 7. lākha, gaḷī, maṇasila, dhāuḍī, mahuḍā, sājī, tūrī, sākaroḍa, bhāṁga, sābū, kandādika vahoryāṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (lac-trade, glossed:) 'may have dealt in lac, indigo, realgar, dhāvaḍī-flowers, mahuā, soda-ash, tūrī, sākaroḍa, hemp, soap, tubers and such'
wordByWord.etymology: gaḷī ('indigo'); maṇasila ('realgar', Skt. manaḥśilā); bhāṁga ('cannabis'); vahoryāṁ ('took up, dealt in').

wordByWord.token: ૮. રસ, મધ, મધાંગ, મધુ, માખણ, વેશણાતણો વિક્રય કીધો હોય
wordByWord.translit: 8. rasa, madha, madhāṁga, madhu, mākhaṇa, veśaṇātaṇo vikraya kīdho hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (rasa-trade, glossed:) 'may have sold liquor, intoxicants, honey, butter and fats'
wordByWord.etymology: madha/madhu ('liquor; honey'); mākhaṇa ('butter').

wordByWord.token: ૯. વિષ, હળ, હથિયાર, લોહયંત્ર, હરિયાલ, કાંકસી પ્રમુખ જીવઘાતક વસ્તુ વેંચી વેંચાવી હોય
wordByWord.translit: 9. viṣa, haḷa, hathiyāra, lohayantra, hariyāla, kāṁkasī pramukha jīvaghātaka vastu veṁcī veṁcāvī hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (poison-trade, glossed:) 'may have sold or caused to be sold life-destroying things — poison, ploughs, weapons, iron-machines, orpiment and such'
wordByWord.etymology: hariyāla ('orpiment', Skt. haritāla); jīva-ghātaka ('life-killing'); veṁcī veṁcāvī ('sold, had sold').

wordByWord.gloss: (kesa/being-trade, glossed:) 'may have traded in bipeds (humans/slaves) and quadrupeds (animals)'
wordByWord.etymology: dvipada ('two-footed'); catuṣpada ('four-footed').

wordByWord.token: ૧૧. અરહટ્ટ, પાવટ્ટ, કોલહું, લોઢણ, ઊખલ, મૂશલ, ઘંટી, ઘાણી વાહ્યા હોય
wordByWord.translit: 11. arahaṭṭa, pāvaṭṭa, kolahuṁ, loḍhaṇa, ūkhala, mūśala, ghaṁṭī, ghāṇī vāhyā hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (press-work, glossed:) 'may have driven the water-wheel, press, sugarcane-mill, thresher, mortar, pestle, hand-mill and oil-press'
wordByWord.etymology: arahaṭṭa ('Persian wheel', Skt. araghaṭṭa); kolhu ('cane-press'); ghāṇī ('oil-mill').

wordByWord.token: ૧૨. દ્વિપદ, ચતુષ્પદ પ્રત્યે આંક, ડાંભ, પાછણાં, કર્ણ, કંબલ, મુષ્કચ્છેદ, પૃષ્ઠિગાલન, નાસાવેધાદિક કીધાં કરાવ્યાં હોય
wordByWord.translit: 12. dvipada, catuṣpada pratye āṁka, ḍāṁbha, pāchaṇāṁ, karṇa, kaṁbala, muṣkacchheda, pṛṣṭhi-gālana, nāsā-vedhādika kīdhāṁ karāvyāṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (mutilation-work, glossed:) 'may have done or had done to bipeds and quadrupeds: branding, marking, ear- and nose-piercing, castration, back-galling and the like'
wordByWord.etymology: ḍāṁbha ('branding'); muṣka-cchheda ('castration'); nāsā-vedha ('nose-piercing').

wordByWord.token: ૧૩. ખડ અન્નતણી ઘણી નિષ્પત્તિભણી દવ દીધાં, દેવરાવ્યાં હોય
wordByWord.translit: 13. khaḍa annataṇī ghaṇī niṣpattibhaṇī dava dīdhāṁ, devarāvyāṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (forest-fire, glossed:) 'for the sake of abundant grass and grain, may have set fires or had them set'
wordByWord.etymology: khaḍa ('grass'); niṣpatti ('yield, production'); dava ('fire'); dīdhāṁ / devarāvyāṁ ('gave / had given').

wordByWord.token: ૧૪. સર શોષાવ્યાં, ભિલ્લ ખેડાવ્યા, ક્યારા ગહરાવ્યા, દ્રહ, તલાવ ફોડાવ્યાં હોય
wordByWord.translit: 14. sara śoṣāvyāṁ, bhilla kheḍāvyā, kyārā gaharāvyā, draha, talāva phoḍāvyāṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (water-drying, glossed:) 'may have had lakes dried, fields tilled, beds deepened, and pools and tanks breached/drained'
wordByWord.etymology: śoṣāvyāṁ ('had dried'); gaharāvyā ('had deepened'); phoḍāvyāṁ ('had burst/breached').

wordByWord.token: ૧૫. શ્વાન, બિડાલ, શૂડા સાલઢી, મોર, કુર્કુટ, દુરાચાર, દાસ, દાસરુ પોષ્યાં... અનેરાં બહુ પાપ વ્યાપાર વ્યવસાય કીધાં હોય
wordByWord.translit: 15. śvāna, biḍāla, śūḍā sālaḍhī, mora, kurkuṭa, durācāra, dāsa, dāsaru poṣyāṁ... anerāṁ bahu pāpa vyāpāra vyavasāya kīdhāṁ hoy
wordByWord.gloss: (rearing-the-immoral, glossed:) 'may have kept dogs, cats, parrots, sālaḍhī, peacocks, cocks, dissolute persons, male and female slaves — and done many other sinful trades and businesses'
wordByWord.etymology: śvāna ('dog'); biḍāla ('cat'); kurkuṭa ('rooster'); durācāra ('the dissolute'); dāsa/dāsaru ('male/female slave'); anerāṁ ('other').

wordByWord.gloss: concerning these fifteen karmādāna
wordByWord.etymology: pannare ('fifteen', Skt. pañcadaśa); viṣe ('in regard to').

wordByWord.gloss: a gross (formal) restriction, with careful restraint (jayaṇā) in subtle matters
wordByWord.etymology: sthūla ('gross') / sūkṣma ('subtle'); jayaṇā ('careful restraint', Skt. yatanā) — the householder vows the gross abstention and exercises vigilance over the subtle.
wordByWord.notes: The sthūla/sūkṣma pairing is the sūtra's recurring axis of measuring faults (see preface).

wordByWord.gloss: among these fifteen transgressions [whatever fault there has been … — closing refrain follows]
wordByWord.etymology: atiyāra-māṁhe ('among the transgressions'); the trailing ૦ marks the elided micchā-mi-dukkaḍaṁ refrain.
wordByWord.notes: The '૦' is the book's abbreviation sign cueing the reciter to complete the standard closing formula by mind, speech and body.

literalTranslation: From the standpoint of livelihood: I renounce harsh occupations — town-guard, jailer and the like — and evil business pursued for a living. From the standpoint of livelihood I now purify the fifteen transgressions of this vow: charcoal-work, forest-work, cart-work, hire-work, digging-work, ivory-trade, lac-trade, liquid-trade, poison-trade, being-trade, press-work, mutilation-work, the setting of forest-fires, the drying of lakes and ponds, and the rearing of the immoral. (1) Charcoal-work: for profit, fire-trades — smelting, smithing, goldsmithing, brazier- and tinker-work, grain-roasting, brick- and lime-kilning, metal-melting — may have been done. (2) Forest-work: grain, cotton, flowers, fruit, leaves may have been sold; grinding and threshing set up; bamboo, creeper and timber cut and split. (3) Cart-work: carts, axles, wheels may have been traded. (4) Hire-work: loads may have been laid on pack-oxen, carriers, camels, bullocks, donkeys, mules and carts. (5) Digging-work: for a living, mines, stone, earth and gravel may have been dug, and cultivation done. (6) Ivory-trade: at the source, tusks, yak-whisks, musk, claws, fur and hides may have been taken. (7) Lac-trade: lac, indigo, realgar, dhāvaḍī, mahuā, soda, tūrī, sākaroḍa, hemp, soap, tubers and such may have been dealt in. (8) Liquid-trade: liquor, intoxicants, honey, butter and fats may have been sold. (9) Poison-trade: poison, ploughs, weapons, iron-machines, orpiment and such life-destroying things may have been sold or caused to be sold. (10) Being-trade: bipeds and quadrupeds may have been traded. (11) Press-work: water-wheel, press, cane-mill, thresher, mortar, pestle, hand-mill and oil-press may have been driven. (12) Mutilation-work: branding, marking, ear- and nose-piercing, castration, back-galling and the like may have been done or had done to bipeds and quadrupeds. (13) Forest-fire: for abundant grass and grain, fires may have been set or had set. (14) Water-drying: lakes may have been dried, fields tilled, beds deepened, pools and tanks breached. (15) Rearing-the-immoral: dogs, cats, parrots, sālaḍhī, peacocks, cocks, the dissolute, and male and female slaves may have been kept, and many other sinful trades carried on. Concerning these fifteen karmādāna there is a gross restriction and careful restraint in the subtle; for whatever transgression among these fifteen — [may my misdeed be in vain].
idiomaticTranslation: Now I turn to my livelihood. I forswear every cruel calling — the constable's, the jailer's, and all the rest — and every evil trade I might pursue for gain; and I lay bare the fifteen forbidden livelihoods of this vow. The fire-trades that scorch countless tiny lives — charcoal, the smith's forge, the goldsmith's and brazier's furnace, the parcher's pan, the brick- and lime-kiln. The plunder of growing things — selling grain, cotton, blossom, fruit and leaf; grinding, threshing, hewing bamboo and timber. The wheelwright's cart, the hire of pack-beasts bowed under loads, the gouging of mine and quarry and field. The traffic in tusk and whisk and musk and hide; in lac and indigo and orpiment and hemp; in liquor, honey, butter and fat; in poison, plough and weapon — all that takes life; and the buying and selling of living creatures themselves. The grinding mill and pressing-stone; the branding, gelding and piercing of helpless beasts; the forests fired for a richer harvest; the lakes and ponds drained dry; and the keeping of brutes and broken men for sordid profit. For all this — these fifteen, gross and subtle, done and abetted — may my misdeed come to nothing.

elaboration.verseByVerseCommentary: This passage completes the seventh vow. The previous verse examined Bhogopabhoga 'from the side of food' (bhojanataḥ); the single word that opens here, karmataḥ ('from the side of work'), pivots the whole confession from what one consumes to how one earns. The fifteen karmādāna are the classical core of Jain professional ethics: five fire-and-earth industries (charcoal, forest, cart, hire, digging), five harmful trades (ivory, lac, liquid/liquor, poison, living beings), and five directly violent operations (machine-pressing, mutilation, forest-arson, draining water-bodies, and breeding creatures for gain). The verse names each first in its terse Prakrit locative — iṅgālakamme … asai posaṇayā — then, uniquely in this sūtra, supplies a vernacular, almost gazetteer-like inventory of the actual occupations a medieval Gujarati layperson would recognize: the līhālā (smith), kaṁsārā (brazier), nīmāha (lime-kiln), the arahaṭṭa water-wheel, the ghāṇī oil-press. The repeated grammar is telling: nearly every gloss ends not in 'I did' but in kīdhāṁ hoy / karāvyāṁ hoy — 'may have been done, may have been had done.' Guilt is admitted in three grades — doing, commissioning, and (in the closing jayaṇā) tolerating — and across the uncertain past, since the reciter cannot be sure which of these countless acts he touched. The closing line refuses easy absolutism: it grants the householder a sthūla-niyama (a gross, workable vow of abstention) while demanding sūkṣma jayaṇā, scrupulous care in the unavoidable fine grain of economic life. The trailing '૦' hands the reciter back to the universal refrain.
elaboration.doctrinalContext: Karmādāna doctrine translates ahiṁsā into economics: because the layperson cannot abandon livelihood as a monk does, Jainism instead ranks occupations by their ārambha (the scale of life-destruction they entail) and asks the śrāvaka to renounce the heaviest. The fifteen are the standard enumeration of the Upāsakadaśāṅga and the śrāvakācāra tradition systematized in Tattvārtha-sūtra ch. 7, where they fall under the atichāras of the Bhogopabhoga-parimāṇa vow. Two principles surface sharply here. First, vicarious and authorized harm counts: veṁcāvī, devarāvyāṁ, karāvyāṁ ('had it sold, had it set, had it done') confess kārita and anumodanā, not merely krita — harm done through agents and approval. Second, the sthūla/sūkṣma calibration embodies the lay path's realism: the householder takes the 'gross' vow he can keep and accepts moral responsibility (jayaṇā) for the subtle harm he cannot wholly escape — a stance distinct from the ascetic's total renunciation.
elaboration.practicalRelevance: Read today, the list becomes a conscience-audit of one's work and investments. The fifteen map readily onto modern equivalents — arms and ammunition (visa), animal husbandry and the leather, ivory and fur trade (danta, kesa, carma), liquor and intoxicants (rasa/bhāṁga), deforestation, mining and quarrying (vaṇa, phoḍī), industrial slaughter and animal mutilation (nillaṁchaṇa). The reciter is asked not only whether he runs such a business but whether he finances, supplies, or profits from one — the kārita/anumodanā test. The closing jayaṇā offers a usable ethic for anyone enmeshed in a complex economy: vow firmly against the gross harms you can avoid, and stay vigilant and minimizing about the subtle harms you cannot.

sources.title: JES 401 — Jain Philosophy and Practice 2 (Level 4): the fifteen Karmādāna

sources.title: The Code of Conduct according to Jainism (śrāvaka-dharma)

notes: The Prakrit karmādāna-names are stable and securely identified; the verse's vernacular Old-Gujarati gloss-words are the harder part. Several trade-names are regional and only approximately renderable: rāṅgaṇa, līhālā, ṭhaṁṭhārā, bhāḍabhuṁjā, nīmāha (item 1); dharī/ūdhī/pai cart-parts (item 3); dhāuḍī, sājī, tūrī, sākaroḍa (item 7); śūḍā sālaḍhī, dāsaru (item 15) — glossed by best contextual sense and flagged accordingly. This verse spells the word અતિચાર (with ca) in its frame, whereas the book's prevailing orthography is અતિયાર (with ya); both are preserved verbatim per §7 of the tradition base. The litany lists સાડીકમ્મે/ભાડીકમ્મે/ફોડીકમ્મે but the numbered glosses rename them શકટકમ્મે/ભાટકમ્મે/ફોટિકમ્મે — these are the same three karmādāna (śakaṭa, bhāṭaka, sphoṭaka), not additional ones. The trailing '૦' after atiyāramāṁhe is the book's abbreviation marker for the elided closing micchā-mi-dukkaḍaṁ refrain, supplied by the reciter. Mātrā, anusvāra and conjunct variants are kept as printed.
